network.overall_strategy: This network uses literature-grounded route classes rather than claiming a single threonine-specific proven synthesis. It centers threonine formation on the most supported prebiotic amino-acid frameworks in the provided research: Strecker chemistry, phosphoro-Strecker chemistry, hydrolysis of aminonitriles, abiotic keto-acid amination/transamination analogs, hydrothermal feedstock generation, cyanosulfidic/surface feedstock preparation, and dry-wet biochemical completion. Because the literature does not provide a fully resolved unique threonine-specific carbon-skeleton installation sequence, the network explicitly marks the C4 hydroxylated aldehyde/aminonitrile branch as speculative but necessary, while keeping all reaction classes aligned with the cited work. Multiple environmental routes converge on shared hubs: formaldehyde, HCN, glyoxylate, acetaldehyde, aminohydroxylated C4 nitrile intermediates, and 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutanoate. Hydrothermal environments generate reduced carbon feedstocks and keto-acid precursors; surface environments generate cyanide-derived, sugar/aldehyde-derived, and phosphoroaminonitrile intermediates; biochemical/prebiotic environments complete hydrolysis, transamination analogs, and wet-dry enrichment to threonine.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases yields formate as an early fixed-carbon product.
reactions.reasoning: Directly follows hydrothermal CO2 reduction chemistry highlighted in the prompt and is consistent with iron-sulfur vent carbon-fixation models; used here as the primary hydrothermal entry to reduced carbon feeding later surface chemistry (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Russell & Martin, 2004).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type generation of formaldehyde and acetaldehyde

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal/subseafloor setting; FTT-like carbon hydrogenation

reactions.mechanism: Successive reduction of C1 carbon oxides on magnetite/iron surfaces generates small aldehydes including formaldehyde and acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The research explicitly identifies magnetite as relevant to FTT prebiotic chemistry and formaldehyde as a precursor in amino-acid synthesis; using this to supply aldehydes avoids unsupported direct HCN generation from formaldehyde and ammonia in one step (McCollom, 2013; Wolman, Miller, Ibanez & Oro, 1971).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal generation of glyoxylate and pyruvate from fixed-carbon vent chemistry

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal gradients; protometabolic carbon-fixation analog

reactions.mechanism: Sequential reduction/condensation network on Fe-mineral surfaces gives small α-keto acids including glyoxylate and pyruvate from reduced C1 inputs.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report specifically identifies glyoxylate and pyruvate as prebiotically relevant protometabolic inputs. Exact stepwise stoichiometry remains unresolved, so this node is a network-level condensation of documented hydrothermal protometabolic generation of these hubs rather than a claim of a single proven elementary reaction (Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2019; Keller, Turchyn & Ralser, 2014).

reactions.name: Surface UV/cyanosulfidic production of HCN feedstock

conditions.other: UV irradiation, anoxic surface pool/hot spring chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical and sulfur-assisted surface reactions convert reduced carbon/nitrogen feedstocks into cyanide-bearing chemistry in a cyanosulfidic context.
reactions.reasoning: The report supports surface/UV chemistry and cyanosulfidic scenarios as relevant feedstock-generating contexts, but does not give a threonine-specific exact elementary step; this connection is therefore explicitly speculative but necessary to link hydrothermal aldehydes to the documented HCN-dependent Strecker and phosphoro-Strecker pathways (Patel et al., 2015; Sutherland, 2016; Wolman et al., 1971).

reactions.name: Surface formose/aldol generation of glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic pool or geothermal field; wet-dry and UV exposure possible

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde undergoes borate-stabilized formose-type oligomerization/aldol chemistry to generate glycolaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The provided research identifies formaldehyde as a key precursor and formose/aldol chemistry as a surface process. Borate stabilization of sugar intermediates is explicitly required by the framework and helps rationalize persistence of glycolaldehyde (Butlerow, 1861; Ricardo, Carrigan, Olcott & Benner, 2004).

reactions.name: Surface oxidation network from glycolaldehyde to glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV-irradiated shallow pool, anoxic but photoredox-active

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oxidation/rearrangement of C2 hydroxyaldehydes furnishes glyoxylate, adding a second route to this hub.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylate is identified in the literature as a central prebiotic input. Exact mineral-specific conversion from glycolaldehyde is not directly documented in the report, so this reaction is a cautious surface-photochemistry connector consistent with the stated relevance of UV chemistry and aldehyde-derived networks (Patel et al., 2015; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker-type assembly of hydroxylated C4 aminonitrile precursor

conditions.other: evaporitic pool or tidal-flat concentration; anoxic, cyanide-rich

reactions.mechanism: Aldehyde condensation establishes a hydroxylated C4 carbonyl framework, followed by imine formation with ammonia and cyanide capture to give an aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the threonine-carbon-skeleton installation step missing in the failed attempt. It is explicitly marked as speculative because the literature supports Strecker chemistry for amino acids and aldehyde/HCN/ammonia feedstocks but does not provide a uniquely established threonine-specific precursor sequence. The reaction is included only because a hydroxylated C4 aminonitrile is the most chemically faithful extension of the documented Strecker class toward threonine (Strecker, 1850; Commeyras et al., 2004; Wolman et al., 1971).

reactions.name: Surface phosphoro-Strecker formation of threonine N-phosphoroaminonitrile precursor

conditions.other: pH 7; neutral phosphoro-Strecker conditions with DAP and HCN

reactions.mechanism: Diamidophosphate activates the aminonitrile-forming manifold to produce an N-phosphoroaminonitrile under neutral conditions.
reactions.reasoning: This directly follows the reported phosphoro-Strecker framework: aldehyde-derived amino-nitrile chemistry with DAP and HCN at pH 7 giving N-phosphoroaminonitriles. Temperature was not given in the report, so it is left as near-ambient rather than invented (Krishnamurthy, Powner & Sutherland, 2018; Kitadai et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Surface conversion of N-phosphoroaminonitrile to aminothioamide

conditions.other: pH 7 with H2S according to phosphoro-Strecker sequence

reactions.mechanism: Sulfurization of the phosphoroaminonitrile manifold yields an aminothioamide intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: This step is taken directly from the phosphoro-Strecker sequence summarized in the research: N-phosphoroaminonitriles react with H2S at pH 7 to produce aminothioamides (Krishnamurthy, Powner & Sutherland, 2018; Kitadai et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Acidic conversion to free threonine aminonitrile precursor

conditions.other: pH 1.5 acidic conversion conditions from phosphoro-Strecker literature

reactions.mechanism: Acid-mediated dephosphorylation/rearrangement of the phosphoro-thioamide manifold furnishes the corresponding free aminonitrile precursor.
reactions.reasoning: The report explicitly states that acidic conditions around pH 1.5 lead to aminonitriles in the phosphoro-Strecker network. This reaction reconnects that literature sequence to the shared aminonitrile hub used by multiple pathways (Krishnamurthy, Powner & Sutherland, 2018; Kitadai et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate and threonine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis during geothermal cycling; aminonitrile hydrolysis can proceed stepwise through amide/acid manifolds

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis yields amino-acid products, while parallel hydrolytic/deamination equilibration accesses the corresponding α-keto acid 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids is intrinsic to Strecker chemistry. Including parallel access to the corresponding α-keto acid provides a literature-aligned bridge to abiotic keto-acid amination/transamination analogs discussed in the report, without collapsing unrelated chemistry into a single unsupported direct step as in the failed attempt (Strecker, 1850; Commeyras et al., 2004; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic abiotic keto-acid amination/transamination analog to threonine

conditions.other: phosphate-buffered or pool-interface conditions; non-enzymatic amination/transamination analog using α-keto acid network

reactions.mechanism: The hydroxylated α-keto acid undergoes abiotic amination/transamination analog chemistry, aided by amino-group donor pools and cyanide-derived activation, yielding threonine.
reactions.reasoning: The report explicitly supports abiotic conversion of α-keto acids to amino acids and notes parallels to biological transamination. Because threonine-specific yields are not available, this step is the cautious keto-acid completion route required to connect the documented α-keto acid framework to the target (Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2019; Huber & Wachtershauser, 2006; sources 6987 and 6944 as summarized in the report).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal FTT to Strecker threonine route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal aldehyde generation feeds surface HCN production and Strecker-type assembly of a hydroxylated C4 aminonitrile followed by hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formate-carbon fixation to Strecker threonine route
pathways.description: CO2 reduced to formate and then to aldehyde/keto-acid pools that ultimately feed the Strecker branch to threonine.

pathways.name: Formose-assisted Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formaldehyde enters surface formose chemistry to generate glycolaldehyde, which contributes to hydroxylated C4 aminonitrile formation and then threonine.

pathways.name: Phosphoro-Strecker direct completion route
pathways.description: A Strecker-built C4 aminonitrile enters the phosphoro-Strecker manifold through DAP activation, then returns to the aminonitrile hub and hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Phosphoro-Strecker through aminothioamide
pathways.description: The phosphoro-Strecker branch proceeds through sulfurization to an aminothioamide before acidic conversion back to aminonitrile and hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Keto-acid completion from aminonitrile hydrolysis branch
pathways.description: The hydroxylated C4 aminonitrile hydrolyzes into a shared threonine/2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate manifold, then abiotic keto-acid amination furnishes threonine.

pathways.name: Glyoxylate-augmented keto-acid route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal glyoxylate and pyruvate pools support α-keto-acid network chemistry that converges with the threonine keto-acid intermediate and then amination.

pathways.name: Surface photochemical glyoxylate convergence route
pathways.description: Surface-generated glycolaldehyde is photochemically converted to glyoxylate, providing a second convergent α-keto-acid feed into the threonine completion manifold.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-phosphoro-Strecker-to-keto-acid hybrid
pathways.description: Hydrothermal feedstocks support surface phosphoro-Strecker chemistry, whose products hydrolyze into the shared keto-acid manifold for final abiotic amination to threonine.

pathways.name: Dry-wet integrated surface-to-biochemical threonine route
pathways.description: Surface concentration and mineral templating generate the hydroxylated C4 aminonitrile, and subsequent biochemical-style hydrolysis/transamination during geothermal cycling selects threonine from the shared intermediate pool.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Reasonable hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical progression, but the key C4 hydroxylated aminonitrile assembly is highly speculative and not well carbon-accounted. The HCN production step is also weakly grounded as written. Final conversion from aminonitrile to threonine is plausible only for hydrolysis, whereas simultaneous production of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate and threonine from a single precursor is chemically unclear.
suggestions: Replace rxn_004 with a better supported cyanide source or treat HCN as an external feedstock.
Specify a concrete aldehyde precursor for the Strecker step leading to threonine aminonitrile.
Separate aminonitrile hydrolysis to threonine from any keto-acid-forming side branch.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrothermal formate generation is literature-consistent, but the conversion of formate plus formaldehyde into both glyoxylate and pyruvate in one step is too compressed and insufficiently justified. Downstream threonine-specific branch remains speculative, limiting confidence.
suggestions: Decompose rxn_003 into better supported intermediate carbon-fixation steps.
Clarify whether pyruvate or glyoxylate truly contribute to threonine backbone formation in this branch.
Add explicit literature-backed conditions and catalysts for each transformation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Formose-derived glycolaldehyde is plausible in surface settings, but the route from glycolaldehyde plus acetaldehyde to a threonine-specific aminonitrile is not established in the cited literature. This pathway is network-connected but chemically underdefined at the key bond-forming step.
suggestions: Provide a mechanistically explicit C4 hydroxyaldehyde precursor before Strecker conversion.
Distinguish generic Strecker plausibility from threonine-specific evidence.
Use literature-supported intermediates such as glycolonitrile or hydroxymethane sulfonate if relevant.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is more literature-aligned because phosphoro-Strecker chemistry with DAP, HCN, pH-dependent conversion, and aldehyde selectivity is specifically supported. However, the upstream generation of the threonine-relevant aminonitrile precursor remains speculative, and the final hydrolysis is oversimplified.
suggestions: Keep the phosphoro-Strecker branch but anchor it to an explicit aldehyde substrate rather than a generic precursor.
State pH progression explicitly for rxn_008-rxn_010 to better match literature.
Model hydrolysis of N-phosphoroaminonitrile through known intermediates instead of direct shortcutting.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Among the proposed routes, this best tracks the literature because phosphoro-Strecker intermediates and H2S-mediated aminothioamide formation are explicitly reported. The major weakness remains the unsupported installation of the exact threonine carbon skeleton before phosphoro-Strecker completion. The terminal hydrolysis logic still needs refinement.
suggestions: Retain the aminothioamide branch as a literature-supported feature.
Improve structural definition and formula consistency of mol_013-mol_015.
Provide a more defensible route to the precursor aldehyde or aminonitrile consistent with threonine stereochemistry and carbon count.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This branch is weak because rxn_011 conflates hydrolysis to threonine with formation of a keto acid, and rxn_012 uses an implausibly crowded set of inputs to reach threonine without a clear mechanistic basis. It resembles a network closure device more than a chemically validated path.
suggestions: Split hydrolysis and transamination branches into separate, chemically explicit sequences.
Use a single defined keto-acid precursor for abiotic amination rather than multiple loosely justified co-inputs.
Cite Fe0-mediated reductive amination style chemistry if invoking keto-acid to amino-acid conversion.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Use of glyoxylate and pyruvate aligns with literature on protometabolic and abiotic keto-acid chemistry, but the final amination/transamination analog to threonine is not sufficiently specific or well balanced. The pathway points in a relevant direction yet does not convincingly deliver the target.
suggestions: Define a specific C4 α-keto acid precursor to threonine and then an amination step.
Avoid using HCN, glyoxylate, pyruvate, NH3, and another keto acid all in one catch-all reaction.
Tie the branch more directly to reported abiotic keto-acid amination conditions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good convergence on glyoxylate from surface chemistry, but the pathway still depends on the speculative threonine-specific Strecker precursor assembly. UV/surface framing is literature-consistent in broad terms, not in the detailed reaction implementation.
suggestions: Strengthen the UV chemistry by using better supported cyanide-generation or cyanosulfidic intermediates.
Clarify whether glyoxylate is a feeder metabolite or a direct carbon-source contributor to threonine.
Make the final branch less schematic.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This hybrid pathway has strong network logic and good environmental interconnection, but several individual steps are too compressed or speculative. It is topologically strong yet chemically only moderately convincing, especially at the threonine-forming end.
suggestions: Keep the cross-environment hybrid concept.
Reduce shortcut reactions and insert experimentally motivated intermediates.
Use either the phosphoro-Strecker completion or keto-acid amination completion, not both in a single overpacked route.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Dry-wet integrated surface-to-biochemical progression is plausible and in line with prebiotic enrichment logic. The strongest part is the phosphoro-Strecker/aminothioamide core. The weakest part remains the origin and exact identity of the threonine-specific aminonitrile precursor and the simplified hydrolysis endpoint.
suggestions: Ground the precursor in a defined hydroxyaldehyde substrate.
Add pH-sensitive conversion details for the phosphoro-Strecker sequence.
Use a single chemically clean terminal step to threonine.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically solid: it has sufficient node counts, multiple hubs, several convergence points, and clear Hydrothermal -> Surface -> Biochemical flow. It also aligns with the literature at the level of route classes: Strecker chemistry, phosphoro-Strecker chemistry, H2S conversion to aminothioamides, hydrothermal feedstock generation, surface photochemistry, and keto-acid/amination analogs are all relevant to published prebiotic threonine discussions. However, the core weakness is chemical specificity. The network does not convincingly establish a literature-supported, threonine-specific C4 hydroxyaldehyde or aminonitrile precursor. Several reactions are overly compressed or act as placeholders, especially rxn_003, rxn_004, rxn_007, rxn_011, and rxn_012. The literature summary itself notes that threonine-specific mechanisms remain unresolved, and the network honestly reflects that, but this limits approval. Mineral-agent compliance cannot be fully confirmed from the summary because specific catalysts/conditions per reaction are not shown here; if omitted in the underlying JSON, that would be a major formal failure. Literature alignment is moderate: good on phosphoro-Strecker and broad prebiotic context, weaker on exact threonine-forming steps and on reaction-specific evidentiary support.
